subject: Physics
question_text: A short bar magnet placed with its axis at 30° with a uniform exernal magnetic field of 0.25 T experiences a torque of magnitude equal to {tex}4.5 \times {10^{ - 2}}J{/tex} . What is the magnitude of magnetic moment of the magnet?
question_html: A short bar magnet placed with its axis at 30° with a uniform exernal magnetic field of 0.25 T experiences a torque of magnitude equal to {tex}4.5 \times {10^{ - 2}}J{/tex}. What is the magnitude of magnetic moment of the magnet?
solution_text: Given, {tex}\theta = 30^\circ {/tex} , B = 0.25 T, {tex}\tau = 4.5 \times {10^{ - 2}}J{/tex} Using formula, {tex}\tau = M B\;\sin \theta {/tex} {tex}\therefore M = \frac{\tau }{{B\sin \theta }} = \frac{{4.5 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}{{0.25 \times \sin 30^\circ }}{/tex} = 0.36 JT -1
solution_html:
Given, {tex}\theta = 30^\circ {/tex}, B = 0.25 T, {tex}\tau = 4.5 \times {10^{ - 2}}J{/tex}
Using formula,
{tex}\tau = M B\;\sin \theta {/tex}
{tex}\therefore M = \frac{\tau }{{B\sin \theta }} = \frac{{4.5 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}{{0.25 \times \sin 30^\circ }}{/tex} = 0.36 JT-1

subject: Physics
question_text: A short bar magnet of magnetic moment m = 0.32 JT -1 is placed in a uniform magnetic field of 0.15 T. If the bar is free to rotate in the plane of the field, which orientation would correspond to its stable, and unstable equilibrium? What is the potential energy of the magnet in each case?
question_html:
A short bar magnet of magnetic moment m = 0.32 JT-1 is placed in a uniform magnetic field of 0.15 T. If the bar is free to rotate in the plane of the field, which orientation would correspond to its

  1. stable, and
  2. unstable equilibrium?

What is the potential energy of the magnet in each case?
solution_text: Magnetic Moment of the bar magnet is given by , M = {tex}0.32 J T^{-1}{/tex} External magnetic field is given by, B = 0.15 T For the system to be in stable equilibrium the angle θ , between the bar magnet and the magnetic field is taken as 0°. Potential energy of the system = {tex}-MB\cos\theta{/tex} {tex}=-0.32 \times 0.15 \cos 0^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}=-4.8 \times 10^{-2} J{/tex} For unstable equilibrium, the bar magnet is oriented at 180°to the magnetic field. θ = 180° Potential energy = – MB cos θ {tex}=-0.32 \times 0.15 \cos 180^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}=4.8 \times 10^{-2} J{/tex}
solution_html:
Magnetic Moment of the bar magnet is given by , M = {tex}0.32 J T^{-1}{/tex} 
External magnetic field is given by, B = 0.15 T

  1. For the system to be in stable equilibrium the angle θ, between the bar magnet and the magnetic field is taken as 0°.
    Potential energy of the system = {tex}-MB\cos\theta{/tex}
    {tex}=-0.32 \times 0.15 \cos 0^{\circ}{/tex}
    {tex}=-4.8 \times 10^{-2} J{/tex}

  2. For unstable equilibrium, the bar magnet is oriented at 180°to the magnetic field.
    θ = 180°
    Potential energy = – MB cos θ
    {tex}=-0.32 \times 0.15 \cos 180^{\circ}{/tex}
    {tex}=4.8 \times 10^{-2} J{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_text: A closely wound solenoid of 800 turns and area of cross-section {tex}2.5 \times {10^{ - 4}}{m^2}{/tex} carries a current of 3.0 A. Explain the sense in which the solenoid acts like a bar magnet. What is its associated magnetic moment?
question_html: A closely wound solenoid of 800 turns and area of cross-section {tex}2.5 \times {10^{ - 4}}{m^2}{/tex} carries a current of 3.0 A. Explain the sense in which the solenoid acts like a bar magnet. What is its associated magnetic moment?
solution_text: Number of turns in the solenoid, n = 800 Area of cross-section, A = 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 m 2 Current in the solenoid, I = 3.0A A current-carrying solenoid behaves as a bar magnet because a magnetic field develops along its axis, i.e., along its length. The magnetic moment associated with the given current-carrying solenoid is calculated as: M = nIA {tex}=800 \times 3 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4}{/tex} = 0.6 J T -1
solution_html:
Number of turns in the solenoid, n = 800
Area of cross-section, A = 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 m2
Current in the solenoid, I = 3.0A
A current-carrying solenoid behaves as a bar magnet because a magnetic field develops along its axis, i.e., along its length.
The magnetic moment associated with the given current-carrying solenoid is calculated as:
M = nIA
{tex}=800 \times 3 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4}{/tex}
= 0.6 J T-1

subject: Physics
question_text: If the solenoid is free to turn about the vertical direction and a uniform horizontal magnetic field of 0.25 T is applied, having the magnetic moment, M = 0.6 T -1 . What is the magnitude of torque on the solenoid when its axis makes an angle of 30° with the direction of applied field?
question_html: If the solenoid is free to turn about the vertical direction and a uniform horizontal magnetic field of 0.25 T is applied, having the magnetic moment, M = 0.6 T-1. What is the magnitude of torque on the solenoid when its axis makes an angle of 30° with the direction of applied field?
solution_text: Magnetic field strength, B = 0.25 T magnetic moment, M = 0.6 T -1 The angle {tex}\theta{/tex} , between the axis of the solenoid and the direction of the applied field, is 30 o . Therefore, {tex}\tau=M B \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}=0.6 \times 0.25 \sin 30^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}=7.5 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{J}{/tex}
solution_html:
Magnetic field strength, B = 0.25 T
magnetic moment, M = 0.6 T-1
The angle {tex}\theta{/tex}, between the axis of the solenoid and the direction of the applied field, is 30o.
Therefore,
{tex}\tau=M B \sin \theta{/tex}
{tex}=0.6 \times 0.25 \sin 30^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}=7.5 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{J}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_text: A bar magnet of magnetic moment 1.5 J T -1 lies aligned with the direction of a uniform magnetic field of 0.22 T. What is the amount of work required by an external torque to turn the magnet so as to align its magnetic moment (i) normal to the field direction, (ii) opposite to the field direction? What is the torque on the magnet in cases (i) and (ii)?
question_html:
A bar magnet of magnetic moment 1.5 J T-1 lies aligned with the direction of a uniform magnetic field of 0.22 T.

  1. What is the amount of work required by an external torque to turn the magnet so as to align its magnetic moment (i) normal to the field direction, (ii) opposite to the field direction?
  2. What is the torque on the magnet in cases (i) and (ii)?
solution_text: Magnetic moment, M = {tex}1.5 J T^{-1}{/tex} Magnetic field strength, B = 0.22 T The work required to align the magnetic moment normal to the direction of magnetic field is given as: {tex}W=-M B\left(\cos \theta_{2}-\cos \theta_{1}\right){/tex} {tex}=-1.5 \times 0.22\left(\cos 90^{\circ}-\cos 0^{\circ}\right){/tex} {tex}=-0.33(0-1){/tex} {tex}=0.33 J{/tex} The work required to make the magnetic moment opposite to the direction of magnetic field ( {tex}\theta_1{/tex} = 0° and {tex}\theta_2{/tex} = 180°) is given as: {tex}W=-M B\left(\cos \theta_{2}-\cos \theta_{1}\right){/tex} {tex}=-1.5 \times 0.22\left(\cos 180^{\circ}-\cos 0^{\circ}\right){/tex} {tex}=-0.33(-1-1){/tex} {tex}=0.66 J{/tex} For case (i): {tex}\theta=\theta_{2}=90^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Torque, {tex}\tau=M B \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}=1.5 \times 0.22 \sin 90^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}=0.33 J{/tex} For case (ii): {tex}\theta=\theta_{2}=180^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Torque, {tex}\tau=M B \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}=M B \sin 180^{\circ}=0 J{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Magnetic moment, M = {tex}1.5 J T^{-1}{/tex} 
    Magnetic field strength, B = 0.22 T
    1. The work required to align the magnetic moment normal to the direction of magnetic field is given as:
      {tex}W=-M B\left(\cos \theta_{2}-\cos \theta_{1}\right){/tex}
      {tex}=-1.5 \times 0.22\left(\cos 90^{\circ}-\cos 0^{\circ}\right){/tex}
      {tex}=-0.33(0-1){/tex}
      {tex}=0.33 J{/tex}

    2. The work required to make the magnetic moment opposite to the direction of magnetic field ({tex}\theta_1{/tex} = 0° and {tex}\theta_2{/tex} = 180°) is given as:
      {tex}W=-M B\left(\cos \theta_{2}-\cos \theta_{1}\right){/tex}
      {tex}=-1.5 \times 0.22\left(\cos 180^{\circ}-\cos 0^{\circ}\right){/tex}
      {tex}=-0.33(-1-1){/tex}
      {tex}=0.66 J{/tex}

  2. For case (i): {tex}\theta=\theta_{2}=90^{\circ}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Torque, {tex}\tau=M B \sin \theta{/tex}
    {tex}=1.5 \times 0.22 \sin 90^{\circ}{/tex}
    {tex}=0.33 J{/tex}
    For case (ii): {tex}\theta=\theta_{2}=180^{\circ}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Torque, {tex}\tau=M B \sin \theta{/tex}{tex}=M B \sin 180^{\circ}=0 J{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_text: A closely wound solenoid of 2000 turns and area of cross section {tex}1.6 \times {10^{ - 4}}{m^2}{/tex} , carrying a current of 4.0 A, is suspended through its centre allowing it to turn in a horizontal plane. What is the magnetic moment associated with the solenoid? What is the force and torque on the solenoid if a uniform horizontal magnetic field of {tex}7.5 \times {10^{ - 2}}T{/tex} is set up at an angle of 30° with the axis of the solenoid?
question_html:
A closely wound solenoid of 2000 turns and area of cross section {tex}1.6 \times {10^{ - 4}}{m^2}{/tex}, carrying a current of 4.0 A, is suspended through its centre allowing it to turn in a horizontal plane.

  1. What is the magnetic moment associated with the solenoid?
  2. What is the force and torque on the solenoid if a uniform horizontal magnetic field of {tex}7.5 \times {10^{ - 2}}T{/tex} is set up at an angle of 30° with the axis of the solenoid?
solution_text: Magnetic moment, M = N I A {tex} = 2000 \times 4 \times 1.6 \times {10^{ - 4}}{/tex} = 1.28 Am 2 The direction of {tex}\overrightarrow M {/tex} is along the axis of the solenoid in the direction related to the sense of current via the right handed screw rule. The magnetic field is given to be uniform. So, the force on the solenoid is zero. Torque, {tex}\tau = MB\;\sin \theta = 1.28 \times 7.5 \times {10^{ - 2}}\sin 30^\circ {/tex} {tex} = 1.28 \times 7.5 \times {10^{ - 2}} \times \frac{1}{2}J = 0.048\;J{/tex} The direction of the torque is such that the solenoid tends to align the axis of the solenoid (magnetic moment vector) along {tex}\overrightarrow B {/tex} .
solution_html:
1. Magnetic moment, M = N I A {tex} = 2000 \times 4 \times 1.6 \times {10^{ - 4}}{/tex} = 1.28 Am2
    The direction of {tex}\overrightarrow M {/tex} is along the axis of the solenoid in the direction related to the sense of current via the right handed screw rule.
  2. The magnetic field is given to be uniform. So, the force on the solenoid is zero.
    Torque, {tex}\tau = MB\;\sin \theta = 1.28 \times 7.5 \times {10^{ - 2}}\sin 30^\circ {/tex}
    {tex} = 1.28 \times 7.5 \times {10^{ - 2}} \times \frac{1}{2}J = 0.048\;J{/tex}
    The direction of the torque is such that the solenoid tends to align the axis of the solenoid (magnetic moment vector) along {tex}\overrightarrow B {/tex}.

subject: Physics
question_text: A short bar magnet has a magnetic moment of 0.48 JT -1 . Give the direction and magnitude of the magnetic field produced by the magnet at a distance of 10 cm from the centre of the magnet on the axis, the equatorial lines (normal bisector) of the magnet.
question_html:
A short bar magnet has a magnetic moment of 0.48 JT-1. Give the direction and magnitude of the magnetic field produced by the magnet at a distance of 10 cm from the centre of the magnet on

  1. the axis,
  2. the equatorial lines (normal bisector) of the magnet.
solution_text: Given M = 0.48 JT -1 , r = 10 cm = 0.1 m Magnetic field at its axis, {tex}{B_a} = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }}\frac{{2M}}{{{r^3}}}{/tex} {tex}{B_a} = \frac{{4\pi \times {{10}^{ - 7}} \times 2 \times 0.48}}{{4\pi \times {{\left( {0.1} \right)}^3}}}{/tex} {tex}{B_a} = 960 \times {10^{ - 7}}{/tex} {tex}T = 960 \times {10^{ - 3}}G{/tex} B a = 0.96G along N-S line. Magnetic field along the equatorial line, {tex}{B_e} = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }}\frac{M}{{{r^3}}}{/tex} {tex}{B_e} = \frac{{4\pi \times {{10}^{ - 7}} \times 0.48}}{{4\pi \times {{\left( {0.1} \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}{B_e} = 480 \times {10^{ - 7}}{/tex} {tex}T = 480 \times {10^{ - 3}}G{/tex} B e = 0.48G along N-S line.
solution_html:
Given M = 0.48 JT-1, r = 10 cm = 0.1 m

  1. Magnetic field at its axis,
    {tex}{B_a} = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }}\frac{{2M}}{{{r^3}}}{/tex}
    {tex}{B_a} = \frac{{4\pi \times {{10}^{ - 7}} \times 2 \times 0.48}}{{4\pi \times {{\left( {0.1} \right)}^3}}}{/tex}
    {tex}{B_a} = 960 \times {10^{ - 7}}{/tex} {tex}T = 960 \times {10^{ - 3}}G{/tex}
    Ba = 0.96G along N-S line.
  2. Magnetic field along the equatorial line,
    {tex}{B_e} = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }}\frac{M}{{{r^3}}}{/tex}
    {tex}{B_e} = \frac{{4\pi \times {{10}^{ - 7}} \times 0.48}}{{4\pi \times {{\left( {0.1} \right)}^2}}}{/tex}
    {tex}{B_e} = 480 \times {10^{ - 7}}{/tex}{tex}T = 480 \times {10^{ - 3}}G{/tex}
    Be = 0.48G along N-S line.

subject: Physics
question_text: A short bar magnet placed in a horizontal plane has its axis aligned along the magnetic north-south direction. Null points are found on the axis of the magnet at 14 cm from the centre of the magnet. The earth’s magnetic field at the place is 0.36 G and the angle of dip is zero. What is the total magnetic field on the normal bisector of the magnet at the same distance as the null–point (i.e., 14 cm) from the centre of the magnet? (At null points, field due to a magnet is equal and opposite to the horizontal component of earth’s magnetic field.)
question_html: A short bar magnet placed in a horizontal plane has its axis aligned along the magnetic north-south direction. Null points are found on the axis of the magnet at 14 cm from the centre of the magnet. The earth’s magnetic field at the place is 0.36 G and the angle of dip is zero. What is the total magnetic field on the normal bisector of the magnet at the same distance as the null–point (i.e., 14 cm) from the centre of the magnet? (At null points, field due to a magnet is equal and opposite to the horizontal component of earth’s magnetic field.)
solution_text: Earth's magnetic field at the given place, H = 0.36G The magnetic field at a distance d, on the axis of the magnetic is given as: {tex}B_{1}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{2 M}{d^{3}}=H{/tex} .... (i) where, {tex}\mu_{0}{/tex} = Permeability of free space M = Magnetic moment The magnetic field at the same distance d, on the equatorial line of the magnet is given as: {tex}B_{2}=\frac{\mu_{0} M}{4 \pi d^{3}}=\frac{H}{2}{/tex} [Using equation (i)] Total magnetic field on the normal bisector is given by, B = B 1 + B 2 {tex}=H+\frac{H}{2}{/tex} = 0.36 + 0.18 = 0.54G Hence, the magnetic field is 0.54 G in the direction of earth's magnetic field.
solution_html:
Earth's magnetic field at the given place, H = 0.36G
The magnetic field at a distance d, on the axis of the magnetic is given as:
{tex}B_{1}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{2 M}{d^{3}}=H{/tex} .... (i)
where,
{tex}\mu_{0}{/tex} = Permeability of free space
M = Magnetic moment
The magnetic field at the same distance d, on the equatorial line of the magnet is given as:
{tex}B_{2}=\frac{\mu_{0} M}{4 \pi d^{3}}=\frac{H}{2}{/tex} [Using equation (i)]
Total magnetic field on the normal bisector is given by, B = B1 + B2
{tex}=H+\frac{H}{2}{/tex}
= 0.36 + 0.18 = 0.54G
Hence, the magnetic field is 0.54 G in the direction of earth's magnetic field.

subject: Physics
question_text: If the bar magnet in exercise 5.13 is turned around by 180°, where will the new null points be located?
question_html: If the bar magnet in exercise 5.13 is turned around by 180°, where will the new null points be located?
solution_text: If the bar magnet is turned by 180 0 then ,The magnetic field on the axis of the magnet at a distance d 1 can be written as: {tex}B_{1}=\frac{\mu_{0} 2 M}{4 \pi\left(d_{1}\right)^{3}}=H{/tex} .... (i) where, M = Magnetic moment {tex}\mu_{0}{/tex} = Permeability of free space H = Horizontal component of the magnetic field at d 1 If the bar magnet is turned through 180 o , then the neutral point will lie on the equatorial line. Hence, the magnetic field at a distance d 2 , on the equatorial line of the magnet can be written as: {tex}B_{2}=\frac{\mu_{0} M}{4 \pi\left(d_{2}\right)^{3}}=H{/tex} .... (ii) Equating equations (i) and (ii), we get: {tex}\frac{2}{\left(d_{1}\right)^{3}}=\frac{1}{\left(d_{2}\right)^{3}}{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{d_{2}}{d_{1}}\right)^{3}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore d_{2}=d_{1} \times\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}{/tex} {tex}=14 \times 0.794=11.1 \mathrm{cm}{/tex} The new null points will be located 11.1 cm on the normal bisector.
solution_html:
If the bar magnet is turned  by 1800 then ,The magnetic field on the axis of the magnet at a distance d1 can be written as:
{tex}B_{1}=\frac{\mu_{0} 2 M}{4 \pi\left(d_{1}\right)^{3}}=H{/tex} .... (i)
where,
M = Magnetic moment
{tex}\mu_{0}{/tex} = Permeability of free space
H = Horizontal component of the magnetic field at d1
If the bar magnet is turned through 180o, then the neutral point will lie on the equatorial line.
Hence, the magnetic field at a distance d2, on the equatorial line of the magnet can be written as:
{tex}B_{2}=\frac{\mu_{0} M}{4 \pi\left(d_{2}\right)^{3}}=H{/tex} .... (ii)
Equating equations (i) and (ii), we get:
{tex}\frac{2}{\left(d_{1}\right)^{3}}=\frac{1}{\left(d_{2}\right)^{3}}{/tex}
{tex}\left(\frac{d_{2}}{d_{1}}\right)^{3}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore d_{2}=d_{1} \times\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}{/tex}
{tex}=14 \times 0.794=11.1 \mathrm{cm}{/tex}
The new null points will be located 11.1 cm on the normal bisector.
